topic: Someone discovers their partner has been writing a novel about them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The scent of aged paper and strong coffee hung heavy in the air of the study. I found the laptop open on the desk, the screen illuminating a meticulously formatted manuscript. My breath hitched, a tight, unpleasant knot forming in my chest. It was *my* life, laid out in such sterile, literary terms. I leaned in, my gaze sweeping over the elegant prose describing my mundane morning routine—the way I brewed tea, the slight furrow in my brow when I struggled with the crossword. A bitter taste rose in my throat. This wasn't love; it was dissection. I straightened up slowly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor. "What is this?" My voice was dangerously level, devoid of warmth. The response, when it came, was a practiced, overly sweet apology that only fueled the coldness settling deep in my core.

[story 2]

I watched him for a long moment, my hands gripping the edge of the kitchen counter until my knuckles were white. The words swam before my eyes: descriptions of my moods, my favorite sweater, the way I hum off-key while doing dishes. It felt like being studied under a microscope, every flicker of my existence cataloged and judged by someone else’s pen. A sharp, acidic feeling burned behind my ribs. I turned away abruptly, the sound of my retreating footsteps echoing in the quiet apartment. "Stop," I managed, the word brittle and sharp. He looked up, and the practiced ease vanished, replaced by a flicker of genuine surprise, quickly masked by a strained smile. I didn't wait for an answer. I walked into the living room, deliberately loud, focusing on the mindless television, letting the silence of my own disapproval fill the space between us.

[story 3]

The discovery was accidental, tucked inside a book I hadn't seen. The narrative was unflinching, bordering on invasive, detailing my insecurities and my ridiculous habits with an almost clinical precision. A wave of profound irritation washed over me, so potent it made my jaw clench. I picked up the manuscript, the weight of the paper feeling heavy, accusatory. I didn't bother reading further. Instead, I tossed it onto the sofa, the thud sounding disproportionately loud in the room. "You have no right," I stated, my tone flat, utterly devoid of the affection I usually reserved for him. He flinched, his eyes widening, a visible shift in his demeanor that confirmed everything I already suspected. I watched him scramble to pick up the scattered pages, the frantic energy on his face grating against my own rigid stillness.
